sentences.text: In Debrecen.
In the [NON-ENGLISH] in Debrecen.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after all those standing up so many times, we had to all leave the building, and we had to stand up, stand outside in rows.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we are standing there-- I'm always standing next to my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the grandmother, we are hiding still the grandmother behind us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think she's still there.
Yes, because we are hiding her.
And there comes this SS.
And their boots were so shiny, clean, very shiny.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And standing straight, and he starts to count us.
Eins, zwei, drei, vier, funf.
And came to me.
And he was with a rubber stick, black rubber stick.
At least it was very dark.
It looked black to me.
And when-- when he was counting me, he hit me here, quite strong, and it was hurting, so I sort of almost fell down.
But then I put myself straight.
What happened at this selection there, there were-- there was a other family from Hajdunanas.
And she came to my mother, this woman.
If my mother would change places with her so she would stay on the other-- because we was put on one side.
She was-- we were put in different places-- this side, this side, this side, wherever they wanted to send us.
And the Germans-- so they-- they didn't tell us what they are going to do.
They just did.
Here, here you stand, there you stand.
So she came and she would like to-- my mother to change place with her, go over on the other side.
And she says, your mother-in-law is there, because the Rosenberg grandmother and my grandfather was on the other side.
And so this woman, from-- she should be staying on this side, where we are so she can be with her family.
So my mother, always, was one day, she says she doesn't know why, but she said she just wanted to ask my Rosenberg grandmother what to do.
So at one point, my mother goes over to my grandmother and she says to her, well, this is what this friend says.
I should change places with her.
What do you think?
What should I do?
So my grandmother, all the way, very angry, very upset, helpless.
So she says, you stay where they put you.
So she said-- my mother said to the friend, no, I'm going to stay here.
And somehow we believe, because of this not changing, somehow we did not go to Auschwitz.
We-- we-- It's just, this was our [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] destination, I guess, that we-- we just-- we didn't change places.
And then from Debrecen, we were walking to Sangeorz.
And most of the people there were taken from Debrecen to Auschwitz.
I don't know where they were going.
The trains were going to Auschwitz there, we find out later on.
There they didn't tell us.
But they were putting on-- some people were from Debrecen.
But us-- and I don't know what the reason.
No-- no-- my mother never find out what's the reason.
They were marching us to Sangeorz.
I think it was Sangeorz.
By foot.
By foot.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And--
Mother and the children.
Somehow, I don't see my grandmother there anymore.
She wasn't there.
So she-- they didn't let the sick go.
They-- they said they're separately.
I don't know.
I don't know.
At this point, I don't see my grandmother.
Rosenberg grandmother was in a different place.
She went straight-- from the cattle train, straight to Auschwitz, into the gas chambers.
That's how it was.
And if we would have gone on the other side, we would have gone with her.
So that's how it was there, that this was our destination.
And I think she-- she interfered.
She just gave the push, stay there where they-- they put you.
Wherever they told you to stay, that's the way-- that's the way you stay, she said.
And the four children.
And we are marching.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We don't know why we were there.
We were there about a week.
Sangeorz.
I don't remember too much about this.
I just remember walking.
The walking was something ever-lasting.
It seems to me, after always the walking, like, I pass out.
Like, there is just blackness.
I don't see anything anymore.
Always I find the walking and the hunger and the thirst, afterwards it's just dark.
I can't figure out what is there.
[SIGHS]
They were guarding us, I don't know who.
But they were with guns.
They were-- I-- you know, this-- the next thing I remember, we are on the cattle train.
We are put on the cattle train like sardines.
Like sardines.
There is no place for-- not even for standing.
We were locked in.
The heat in the daytime, the standing for hours, the train is not moving.
Then it moves a little bit.
And standing, standing.
No food, no-- no water, no nothing.
I don't know where was the washroom, but there was no washroom.
My brother tells me there was a hole.
A few holes were made in the cattle car.
I don't know.
I just know that it's-- I'm suffering.
I know that I'm suffering, and I'm desperate.
I have this feeling, which is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very scary.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and those cattle trains, they really have-- you know, they have just the doors.
And then when they close it, they slide it in and they close it with this hinge.
And the lock, they lock it.
And then, they have on one end of the train, what I remember, a little square window with bars, very small.
And there was another one on the other side.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometimes [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] people got desperate and they were making noise, especially one of the ladies.
She started to get hysterical.
And the noise was very big.
So the gun came in through this little window.
And in German, it was said, if you're not going to shut up, I will shoot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And even there, I started to laugh, because this lady, who was getting hysterical because there was no place, no food, no nothing, and-- scary.
So when the gun was pushed through the window, so she turns to her husband in a shrieky voice-- shrieky voice she says, [INAUDIBLE], why don't you do something and tell him to-- don't point that gun?
So we started to laugh.
Is she crazy or something?
She must have been going a little bit nuts already.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I remember on the train-- one time I remember that after a very long time-- it must've been days-- they put in such a small breads.
It was little loafs of bread, few.
We are about 80 people on the train, at least.
I'm not sure of the numbers, but that's what people said that so many went into a cattle train.
And a little bit water they gave us, and a little bit of-- a bite, perhaps, for everybody, or two bites.
It's not enough to sustain us.
It's not enough.
Nothing is enough there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at nighttime, I remember, well, we are tired, of course, standing and standing.
There is no place to sit down.
You see, there is no place to sit.
So how we figured out, pushing this way and that way, my mother sat on her bundle.
We have this little bundle.
We still have this little floury mixture with us.
And my mother gives us like a half a teaspoon for everybody.
That's how we lived.
And this tiny bit of bread, what they give us there, with a little of water.
And then she figured out, because we are becoming cranky-- especially me, and I cannot stand anymore, and I'm tired and I'm just starting to cry.
So she figured out, she's going to sit on this bundle.
And she sat down.
And somehow, we should push ourselves together very good so we can put our heads, our foreheads on her lap.
So that's how we-- and she was, you know, stroking us, like this.
And I wake up and I don't know, a little coat was on us-- on all four of us.
And at night, it was cold.
I was cold, probably from the hunger also.
I was cold.
And I put my head up, and my mother just puts it down and strokes it, just sleep, sleep.
Sometimes I said, I'm hungry.
She says, just sleep.
Or she gives us a little piece of the sugar cube.
You had to be very careful.
It should last for a long time.
We didn't know how long until somebody gives us food.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, at one point, this train, when it's going, going, and it stops-- never went for very long.
Dark.
Bomb.
The bombs were falling.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So black dark, you can't see.
Absolutely, you can't see a thing.
You can't see your hands in front of you.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
In Debrecen, before we went to Sangeorz, we were in this big, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] huge place.
I don't know what kind of place it was.
We had to get naked.
We had to take our clothes off.
Everything, stark naked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were putting-- they were disinfecting us with petroleum.
They washed our hair with this, underarm, private parts.
Everywhere where hair is growing, it has to be washed.
Then, and still naked, they had to-- they were taking our fingerprints.
For what, for what, I don't know.
Nothing was explained to us.
And then a-- like a sheet or something, a blue sheet was given.
And in this nakedness, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my thoughts-- first of all, I was-- I don't know.
I was-- I was shocked to see so much naked bodies.
I never seen naked bodies.
It was amazing.
Shocking, scary-- very scary by that time.
And I see that people try to cover themselves.
And I was wondering, how in heaven can-- how in heaven can they cover themselves with the two hands so they should cover where they're supposed to be covered?
And my mind was just going on this and this.
And I just was watching their body.
And I just say, how, how, if she's covering herself up here, how will she cover herself on the bottom, because every time she moved her hand, something was showing.
And it was fascinating and frightening-- terrible frightening.
And but still my mind was on this nakedness, how it's going to be covered.
I couldn't figure it out.
Couldn't figure it out.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And when, after the fingerprinting, the sheet was given, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my mother-- I don't know.
We were there, and then we went to another room.
And that's where we got our clothes.
So from this on, I don't know how it comes, first the selection or this nakedness.
It must be the nakedness first and then-- so here I go back to the train now.
No.
Nothing, absolutely nothing was told.
We were not informed whatsoever.
We were-- at this point, I felt that we were treated like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nothing.
Like nothing.
Like you treat nothing.
Like we are not human beings.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we are on this train.
So this train-- we were-- we were taken to Sangeorz.
And from Sangeorz, they took us, put us on the train.
On this train we are, and I was saying it so far-- all this story.
So it stops.
It's very dark at night.
And the only light, it lights up when the bomb is falling, and it comes with a tremendous light, so it lights up the sky.
It was fascinating for me.
Again, how can it be so-- and I was waiting for again and again it should come.
I didn't know it's a bomb.
How would I know what is a bomb?
So we must have been close to Vienna.
Somewhere there we were.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we reached-- afterwards we find out we reached, with this train, east of Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But then, again, we were going back to Debrecen.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And in Debrecen, we were selected again.
And by that time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they said we are going to go to work.
That much they said.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So on the train again.
So this train ride, you see, it's all six-- it takes six days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's a few times they were bombadeering.
And the SS-- you should ask me where the SS were.
They were scared.
They went under the train.
They were-- they were scared for their own skins.
But we were locked into the cattle train.
What can we do?
And it's dark, terrible dark.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Helpless.

But they were under the train, because we looked-- the people looked out on the little windows there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they didn't hear the-- the SS walking, or, you know, the shining of the gun.
You know, there is a steel part.
Yeah.
You heard nothing.
Silence.
Just the bomb.
And then, this went on for a long time.
And then it started to get dawn.
And where do you think they were coming out from?
From under the train.
They weren't the brave soldiers we thought, you know, they're supposed to be.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And--
The train did go to near Vienna then.
This is when--
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
We went-- we went-- we went first to-- we arrived to-- I think to Strasshof.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Strasshof we find my grandmother again, she-- because she's with us again.
Safar grandmother.
I never seen my Rosenberg grandparents again.
Rosenberg grandmother had gone to Auschwitz.
Never, ever again.
Never, ever again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were killed in Auschwitz.
And my mother was very happy to see her mother.
But my grandmother was very weak.
And my mother said we should stay with grandmother.
And she still had this little floury mix.
And there was a--
It wasn't-- it wasn't a camp.
This was just outside-- outside.
Because we-- I don't know how long we were there.
We weren't there too long.
But we-- I think we just stopped there for a little while.
And there was this long trench, deep.
To me, it seemed very deep, very wide, and, like, with-- with-- how do you call it?
With cement.
It was, you know, lined with cement, so it wasn't earth.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And people started to go down there, and they said they are going to make a little fire and starting to cook something.
Everybody tried to do something, whatever they still had left.
And my mother said, a little bit of soup for my grandmother, because she needs some nourishing.
Because in Debrecen, they took her medication away from her.
They took everything away from her.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And at one point-- but you see, I notice-- this is also a field.
There's a smaller building where my grandmother is leaned against.
You know, we put her against there.
And there is this trench.
And there is a very big building, far away, very far.
To me, it seemed very far, and the building seemed very tall.
It's grayish.
But a good building, not just any kind.
It's a nice one.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I'm-- I didn't listen again.
I didn't stay with grandmother.
My other siblings did, but I-- I had to see what's going on.
And then I'm very anxious, because everybody's scared.
They were saying, hurry up.
Hurry up before they come-- before the German comes.
Hurry up.
Hurry up.
Because they left-- sort of they left us there.
And-- and I was worried that my mother should already be able to do something, what she wanted, with this cooking.
And then suddenly, somebody says, look, they're coming.
And I'm-- my heart in my throat and I start to tell my mother, come on out, come on out.
It wasn't so easy to come out from this trench, because it was deep.
Somebody had to, you know, put a hand down and help you out.
And most of the people got out.
But three of them-- I think three of them, but maybe there was more.
But three, somehow, I remember, they just couldn't get out.
The rest of them were already too scared to stay there and help.
Everybody was just running back to their little bundles.
And again, I saw something fascinating.
And with my child's mind, I-- something-- I always find something fascinating to watch.
I turn around, at the trench, and I couldn't move.
Why couldn't I move?
Because I said in my mind, how on earth can you run so fast?
Now, I see this time not just men.
I see women also-- and we were told they were Ukrainians-- stick in their hands-- rubber stick in their hands-- and they are running so fast.
But how fast they are running?

What I see, their legs-- I'm not watching their faces anymore, because I'm watching their legs, because their legs were going such a fast, one after another, like you take a camera, you know, and you make the video forward, back.
One after other there is-- you can't even blink how fast the feet is changing.
And I was fascinated with this, just absolutely fascinated how a human being can run so fast.
Then they reach very fast, very fast this trench there.
And they are starting to chase those people who were left in the-- in this ditch there, in this long ditch.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were running like cat and mouse.
The people with their-- with their rubber stick, of course, is the cat.
And the people down there, the little mouse.
But they wanted to come out on one side, crawling, jumping up there, and you know how you like to go up.
Fall back.
As soon as they started to crawl, there was already the stick, hitting them, screaming.
My mother comes.
She says, come away.
Don't watch.
So I don't.
This is the picture, though, what I remember.
I remember a lot of walking.
I remember a terrible lot of walking.
I remember at one-- I don't know where we are walking.
But one walking, it took very long.
I'm extremely tired.
Running.
I mean, from-- I'm very tired from the walking, hungry, thirsty.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And my grandmother has to be carried.
So how do you carry a person who cannot walk?
So my oldest brother and my mother, they hold their hands like this.
They hold onto each other.
And my mother-- my grandmother-- three of us, we put my grandmother up on those hands.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And my mother-- grandmother was wearing long skirt.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She always wore long clothes.
So she was very worried and she was always telling my mother in Yiddish, my child, put me down.
Just leave me here.
I'm already-- you can't do anything with me.
You just go, hold the children.
And my mother was crying.
And she says, mother, don't tell me this, because I'm not going to put you down and I'm not going to leave you anywhere.
Long as I can carry you, I'm going to carry you.
But my grandmother was very worried.
So then she-- she said to us, the three of us in the back, she says, you hold onto my skirt.
And each time I call your names, that person who is called, you have to give a tug on the skirt so I can feel it.
And each time she was saying the names-- our names-- we tugged so she knew that we are there, because she couldn't turn back.
She was-- she was helpless.
And my mother, she was also, are you here?
Are you here?
But there was already not much energy to do much more than just to carry her.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is one picture what I remember.
Then my grandmother was taken from us again.
We arrived to a place-- and she was taken.
The sick was taken away from us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we arrive into a-- we arrive after Gostling--
Well, we find grandmother a few-- one more time.
We find her in St.
Valentin.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we arrived to St.
Valentin, this was a lovely, lovely place.
And also, we always had to walk from this train-- when we went with the train, from the train stations we always had to walk.
And they were long walks.
And it's mountain.
You know, Austria is a lot of mountains.
It's a lovely place.
And they are taking us to a prison, many more people.
And there is a place in this-- it's like a huge, huge swimming pool.
I never in my life I have seen swimming pool before.
But the water wasn't in there.
Around the swimming pool there were beautiful paving, such a colored stones.
I don't what kind, but they were nice.
And after there, there were grass, nice patch of grass all around.
And then, when-- after the grass, nice cabanas, very nice.
So this was a resort.
A resort.
Must have been a very fancy-- to me, it looked-- I'd never seen such a beautiful place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And somebody-- because there were people there before us-- and somebody said to my mother, Safar [INAUDIBLE], your mother is here.
So we are looking.
And she said which-- which of those cabanas.
There were those cabanas are all attached to each other, like, you know, glued together.
Nice.
I go in there.
The first thing-- my grandmother is there, turned to the wall.
She was-- she was hardly living.
Nobody gave her not even water, nothing.
For days she was-- and we don't know how long she was there.
But she was already-- and my mother says, look, I'm here.
And she didn't talk anymore.
She was just-- my mother says, I'm here.
Look, I'm here.
Look.
Open your eyes.
I'm here.
She acknowledged her, that she's there.
And my mother tried to clean her up a little.
And there wasn't much to do really, because we didn't have anything.
You know, again, we had that little bit of sugar.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but when we walk into this room, what fascinated me again-- always find something fascinating-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wooden parquet, shiny, and the sun.
And there is a big window it comes through.
It's a very pretty picture, very empty.
There was nothing but my grandmother against the wall on the floor.
But the floor was very pretty, so I noticed this.
Well, I don't know how long we were here.
But I like the idea there was a-- the windows were low.
And facing the pool, there was also a window.
And I look out on that window, and it was very interesting.
I always like to watch people.
And there I see from the other side there comes a mother and two daughters.
It's very-- I noticed them right away because they seemed to me very tall and very good-looking.
They were pretty people, but so big.
And I see them.
And I said, what are they doing?
They had a-- a scarf, [INAUDIBLE], like a blanket, not even-- a blanket, but a pretty blanket, a colorful one, they have in their hand.
I said, I wonder what they are going to do.
You see, I could always within my mind-- I could always entertain myself.
And I-- We'll have to pick up.
OK.
I shouldn't forget this.
I just--
